author_background: Former marine biologist and university professor specializing in coral reef ecosystems. Married to Lena for 12 years, father to 7-year-old Maya. Was leading a research expedition in the Florida Keys when the outbreak began. Dreamed of establishing marine conservation programs that would protect vulnerable species for future generations.
location: Abandoned aquarium research facility in Marathon, Florida. The building smells of salt, decay, and desperation. Faded educational posters about marine life peel from water-damaged walls. The main tank, once filled with vibrant coral and tropical fish, now holds only murky water and the occasional shadow of something that shouldn't be there.
entry: The generators failed again last night. In the sudden silence, I could hear them scratching at the reinforced glass of the main exhibit hall. Their wet, dragging footsteps echoed through the corridors like some terrible tide. Lena would have known how to fix the generators. She was always the practical one—could repair anything from a leaky faucet to a broken heart. God, I miss her. I miss the way she'd hum while making coffee, the way Maya's laughter would fill our small apartment. Sometimes I still wake up expecting to hear them. Then I remember the last video call—Lena's panicked voice telling me to stay away, that the cities weren't safe. That was 186 days ago. Today I found a family of three hiding in the dolphin observation area. The father was already showing symptoms—that glassy-eyed stare, the way his head twitched to the side. They begged me for help. I had to make the choice Lena would have wanted me to make. I sealed the observation room. I can still hear the mother pounding on the glass, her cries muffled by three inches of acrylic. What kind of monster have I become? The kind that survives, I suppose. The kind Lena would barely recognize.
condition: Physically exhausted with deep circles under bloodshot eyes. Left forearm bears a healing bite mark from two weeks ago—constantly checking for infection. Hands tremble slightly from caffeine withdrawal and stress. Mentally frayed, experiencing vivid dreams of his family that blur with reality upon waking. Voice has grown hoarse from lack of use.
discoveries: The infected are drawn to sound more than sight—learned this after dropping a wrench and nearly being overrun. Saltwater seems to slow their decomposition but doesn't stop them. Found notes from other researchers who were studying the outbreak—they believed it started as a waterborne pathogen that mutated. The aquarium's emergency water filtration system can produce drinkable water if properly maintained.
warnings: Never travel during heavy rain—the noise masks approaching threats. Always carry multiple light sources. Trust no one who won't show their arms and neck. The infected can remain dormant in water for days. If you hear clicking sounds, run—it means they've found something alive.
last_meal: Canned tuna and stale crackers scavenged from the staff breakroom, eaten 8 hours ago. Drank rainwater collected from the roof drainage system.
companions: Currently alone. Was traveling with two other survivors—Marcus and Sarah—until they attempted to raid a supply boat three weeks ago. Watched from the shore as the infected swarmed the vessel. No one surfaced. Still carries Sarah's unfinished sketchbook in his pack.
